title: Traveling Salesman (5 cities)
summary: Visit every city exactly once on a round trip, minimizing total distance.
problem_statement: I have 5 cities. The distance between each pair is: (1,2)=10, (1,3)=15, (1,4)=20, (1,5)=25, (2,3)=12, (2,4)=18, (2,5)=22, (3,4)=14, (3,5)=19, (4,5)=11. Start and end at city 1, visit every other city exactly once. Find the order with minimum total distance.
real_world_example: A delivery driver planning a route through five stops, a tour guide visiting five sites in one day, or a circuit board routing five solder points with one continuous trace.

expected_pattern.variables: x_{c,p} in {0, 1} — whether city c is visited at position p in the tour
expected_pattern.objective: minimize sum over consecutive positions of distance(city at p, city at p+1)
expected_pattern.constraints: for each city: visited at exactly one position; for each position: exactly one city is there

expected_solution_summary: Optimum total distance is 72, achieved by several equivalent tours including 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 1 (10+12+14+11+25 = 72). Reversed tours have the same length, so there are at least two optimal answers.
learning_notes: TSP is the canonical 'permutation via one-hot' problem. n × n binaries encode a tour of n cities, with two families of equality constraints (each city visited once, each position filled once). The quadratic objective couples consecutive positions. This template is the gateway to vehicle routing and scheduling — both lean on the same encoding pattern.
